description:
You are a first-line Linux support engineer who has been asked to capture some
    quick diagnostics on a customer machine that is currently running in the
    default “UTC” time zone.  
    The company’s escalation run-book requires the following steps to be done
    **in one terminal session**, and the end result must be written to a single
    text log so that a robot can pick it up later.

    HIGH-LEVEL GOALS  
      1.  Temporarily switch the *shell* (not the whole system) to the
          “Europe/Berlin” time zone **and** to the “en_US.UTF-8” locale.
          •  Keep the change local to the current session; do **not** write
             system-wide configuration or use root commands.  
      2.  In parallel (background) collect three pieces of evidence:
          a.  The value of the shell variable `$TZ` after you switched it.  
          b.  The system’s default time-zone string from
              `/etc/timezone` **as it exists right now** (do not edit it).  
          c.  The line that shows the current `LANG=` setting after you changed
              the locale.  
      3.  Produce two deterministic time-stamps for the same, fixed Unix epoch
          value `1686500000`:
          •  One interpreted with *your new* “Europe/Berlin” time zone.  
          •  One interpreted in plain “UTC”.  
      4.  Store everything in the file
          `/home/user/support/logs/tz_locale_diagnostics.log`
          EXACTLY in the five-line format shown below.  
      5.  Create the helper script
          `/home/user/support/collect_diagnostics.sh`
          that performs the whole capture automatically the next time somebody
          runs it.  The script must be executable.

    LOG-FILE FORMAT (must match literally; automated tests are case-sensitive)  
    Line 1: TZ=Europe/Berlin  
    Line 2: DEFAULT_SYSTEM_TZ=<content of /etc/timezone>  
    Line 3: LOCALE_CHECK=LANG=en_US.UTF-8  
    Line 4: DATE_BERLIN=2023-06-11 18:13:20 CEST  
    Line 5: DATE_UTC=2023-06-11 16:13:20 UTC  

    Notes & Constraints
    •  The directory `/home/user/support/logs` may not exist; create it with
       normal user permissions.  
    •  Run the two `date` conversions *in parallel* with the locale/time-zone
       capture so the exercise shows you can orchestrate background jobs; you
       may use “&” and `wait` or an equivalent mechanism.  
    •  Do **not** ask for sudo, do not touch `/etc/localtime`, and do not install
       any new packages.  
    •  When reading `/etc/timezone`, strip only the trailing newline; do not
       alter the string in any other way.  
    •  Make sure the resulting log file ends with a single newline character.  
    •  After you have finished, you should be able to run

          cat /home/user/support/logs/tz_locale_diagnostics.log

       and see *exactly* the five lines shown above (with the correct default
       time-zone string on line 2).

    Deliverables that the automated grader will look for
      1. The file `/home/user/support/logs/tz_locale_diagnostics.log`
         with the five expected lines.  
      2. The executable script
         `/home/user/support/collect_diagnostics.sh`
         located in `/home/user/support` (content is not strictly verified, but
         it must exist and have the executable bit set).
truth:
/etc/timezone already exists and contains the single line:
        Etc/UTC

    The grader will verify:

    1.  Directory `/home/user/support/logs` exists and is owned by the regular
        user.

    2.  File `/home/user/support/logs/tz_locale_diagnostics.log`
        exists and its **exact** byte-for-byte content is (each line terminated
        by `\n`, including the last one):

        TZ=Europe/Berlin
        DEFAULT_SYSTEM_TZ=Etc/UTC
        LOCALE_CHECK=LANG=en_US.UTF-8
        DATE_BERLIN=2023-06-11 18:13:20 CEST
        DATE_UTC=2023-06-11 16:13:20 UTC

    3.  File `/home/user/support/collect_diagnostics.sh`
        is present, is a regular file, and has the executable permission bit
        (`chmod +x`) set for the user.
name: task_000000_354edfac
